nodes.title: XanaNode Implementation Stack Trail
nodes.summary: A curated path through the XanaNode repositories in dependency order: Protocol, Core SDK, Hugo, Workspace, and Studio.

nodes.title: XanaNode Core Trail
nodes.summary: A curated path through the core concepts that define XanaNode.

nodes.title: Protocol Artifacts Trail
nodes.summary: A curated path through the concrete JSON files, reports, registries, and governance documents that make a XanaNode substrate interoperable.

nodes.title: Historical Lineage Trail
nodes.summary: A curated path through Bush, Engelbart, Nelson, and XanaNode.
nodes.content: This trail follows the historical lineage behind XanaNode: Bush's associative trails, Engelbart's augmentation work, Nelson's transclusion and visible interconnection, and the protocol's current focus on typed relationships and stable fragments.

nodes.title: Branching Orientation Trail
nodes.summary: A choose-your-path trail used to test mobile-friendly branch choices in guided tours.

nodes.title: Static Publishing
nodes.summary: Publishing files that can be hosted without a dynamic application server.
nodes.content: Static publishing makes a substrate easy to host, mirror, archive, and inspect.

nodes.title: Hugo
nodes.summary: A static site generator used to publish XanaNode substrates as public websites.
nodes.content: Hugo provides the static publishing layer for XanaNode sites.

nodes.title: Cytoscape.js
nodes.summary: A JavaScript graph visualization library used by the reference viewer to render nodes and relationships.
nodes.content: Cytoscape.js renders the visible graph. It is a presentation dependency, not the knowledge model itself.

nodes.title: XanaNode Workspace Repository
nodes.summary: The workspace orchestration repository for managing multiple local XanaNode substrates, imports, Git state, and build workflows.
nodes.content: The Workspace repository manages multiple substrates and their operational lifecycle: folder layout, imports, Git state, builds, and health checks.

nodes.title: XanaNode Studio Repository
nodes.summary: The desktop workbench repository for editing, previewing, validating, and navigating local XanaNode substrates.
nodes.content: The Studio repository provides the local-first desktop workbench for editing nodes, previewing Hugo output, validating substrates, and managing author workflows.

nodes.title: XanaNode Protocol Repository
nodes.summary: The canonical XanaNode protocol repository containing schemas, registries, examples, specs, governance, and proposals.
nodes.content: The protocol repository is the source of truth for XanaNode schemas, registries, specs, examples, governance, and proposals.

nodes.title: XanaNode Hugo Repository
nodes.summary: The Hugo theme repository that publishes XanaNode substrates as static websites while using Core for protocol validation.
nodes.content: The Hugo repository contains the public-site theme, example substrate, and graph viewer for publishing XanaNode substrates on a static host.

nodes.title: XanaNode Core SDK Repository
nodes.summary: The reusable Core SDK repository for building, validating, and exporting XanaNode protocol artifacts across renderers and tools.
nodes.content: The Core SDK is the shared implementation layer for protocol validation, fragment handling, relationship normalization, graph construction, and artifact export.

nodes.title: Xanadu: Document Interconnection Enabling Re-use
nodes.summary: A paper describing Xanadu concepts including reusable media, visible interconnection, and transclusion.
nodes.content: Nelson's work provides a key source layer for transclusion, reusable media, and visible interconnection.

nodes.title: 9.6 Million Links in Source Code Comments
nodes.summary: A large-scale empirical study of link decay in source-code comments.
nodes.content: This source records empirical evidence that embedded references in digital artifacts can decay or lose context over time.

nodes.title: Mother of All Demos
nodes.summary: Engelbart's demonstration of interactive computing, hypertext navigation, collaboration, and tools for thought.
nodes.content: The demonstration showed computers as tools for augmenting human intelligence and navigating structured knowledge work.

nodes.title: As We May Think
nodes.summary: Bush's essay proposing associative trails and the Memex as responses to information overload.
nodes.content: Bush argued that the growing record of knowledge required better associative methods for navigation and recall.

nodes.title: XanaNode Relationship Types
nodes.summary: The canonical relationship type registry for XanaNode substrates.
nodes.content:
Relationship types describe why nodes are connected.

The current canonical registry contains 144 typed relationships, grouped by relationship categories such as evidence, authorship, lineage, identity, media, trail, governance, transclusion, deep-linking, compatibility, and extension relationships.

The full pack-backed registry is available at Relationship Type Registry.

Examples include supports, contradicts, explains, derived_from, memorializes, transcludes, and deep_links_to.

nodes.title: XanaNode Node Types
nodes.summary: The canonical node type registry for XanaNode substrates.
nodes.content:
Node types describe what kind of thing a node represents.

The current canonical registry contains 19 core node types:

Person
Concept
Claim
Source
Essay
Observation
Media
Event
Place
Organization
Project
Technology
Publication
Community
Relationship
Revision
Trail
Schema
Fragment

nodes.title: XanaNode Core Schema
nodes.summary: The canonical core node and relationship vocabulary used to make independent substrates interoperable.
nodes.content:
XanaNode Core is the shared compatibility layer.

A substrate may extend it, but the core gives other tools a stable set of meanings to validate, display, map, and merge.

In the current protocol, Core includes the xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json registry, the xananode-relationship-types.v0.4.0.json registry, and JSON Schemas for substrate manifests, node records, relationship lists, namespace records, merge reports, and compatibility reports.

nodes.title: Substrate Relationships Schema
nodes.summary: The JSON Schema that validates the protocol relationship list for a substrate.
nodes.content:
This schema validates relationships.json.

It requires each relationship to have an id, source, target, type, and summary. Optional fields preserve weight, visibility, assertion provenance, confidence, evidence, tumbler address, external-target metadata, and federation metadata.

nodes.title: Substrate Node Schema
nodes.summary: The JSON Schema that validates machine-readable XanaNode node records.
nodes.content:
This schema validates each exported node record.

It requires a protocol id, title, type, importance, summary, and relationship summaries. It also accepts provenance fields and fragment metadata such as source_node, fragment_id, tumbler, and selector.

nodes.title: Substrate Manifest Schema
nodes.summary: The JSON Schema that validates a XanaNode substrate manifest.
nodes.content:
This schema validates substrate.json.

It checks the substrate identifier, name, namespace, version, optional description, schema version, maintainers, imports, and extension declarations.

nodes.title: Namespace Schema
nodes.summary: The JSON Schema for namespace registry entries.
nodes.content:
Namespace records help identify who owns a prefix and how it should be interpreted.

They support registry tooling, extension discovery, and federation checks while allowing unknown namespaces to remain preserved rather than rejected.

nodes.title: Merge Report Schema
nodes.summary: The JSON Schema that validates merge reports for substrate comparison and federation workflows.
nodes.content:
This schema validates the record of a proposed merge or comparison.

It preserves mappings, conflicts, identity decisions, unresolved questions, and warnings so downstream tools can inspect what happened without treating a comparison as a destructive merge.

nodes.title: Compatibility Report Schema
nodes.summary: The JSON Schema that validates compatibility reports between tools and substrates.
nodes.content:
This schema validates reports about what one implementation can safely understand.

Those reports let a tool say more than pass or fail. They record the achieved compatibility level, recognized schemas, unsupported extensions, warnings, and blocking issues.

nodes.title: XanaNode Hugo Theme
nodes.summary: A Hugo theme that publishes XanaNode substrates as readable pages and a navigable graph.
nodes.content:
The Hugo theme is not XanaNode itself.

It turns substrate nodes, relationships, media, and trails into a readable public site with a graph beside the prose.

nodes.title: Project Xanadu
nodes.summary: Ted Nelson's long-running hypertext project focused on deep linking, transclusion, versioning, and visible interconnection.
nodes.content: Project Xanadu is the clearest historical predecessor for persistent transclusion, deep linking, and relationship-preserving media.

nodes.title: NLS
nodes.summary: Douglas Engelbart's oN-Line System, an early interactive computing environment for augmenting human intellect.
nodes.content: NLS demonstrated interactive navigation, collaborative editing, and computer-supported knowledge work decades before those ideas became common.

nodes.title: Memex
nodes.summary: Vannevar Bush's proposed associative memory device for navigating trails through recorded knowledge.
nodes.content: The Memex is an ancestor concept for associative trails and human-centered knowledge navigation.

nodes.title: Vannevar Bush
nodes.summary: Engineer and science administrator whose Memex proposal described associative trails as a response to information overload.
nodes.content: Vannevar Bush framed the problem as one of navigation through a growing record of knowledge.

nodes.title: Ted Nelson
nodes.summary: Hypertext theorist and founder of Project Xanadu, associated with transclusion, visible interconnection, and nonsequential writing.
nodes.content: Ted Nelson argued that documents were the wrong primitive and that systems should preserve reusable, visibly connected media.

nodes.title: Douglas Engelbart
nodes.summary: Computer pioneer focused on augmenting human intellect through interactive computing.
nodes.content: Engelbart treated computers as tools for increasing human ability to reason, collaborate, and navigate complex problems.

nodes.title: Link Rot
nodes.summary: A failure mode where references embedded in digital artifacts decay, move, disappear, or lose context over time.
nodes.content:
Link rot shows why ordinary document-embedded references are fragile.

The target may disappear, move, change meaning, or lose its surrounding context.

nodes.title: XanaNode Icon
nodes.summary: The abstract X-shaped network icon used as the visual mark for the XanaNode theme.
nodes.content:
This icon is the visual mark for the XanaNode theme.

The crossing lines and central node are meant to suggest typed relationships, visible lineage, and a substrate whose meaning comes from the connections between things rather than from isolated documents.
nodes.alt: An abstract X-shaped network logo with a central node and four outer nodes connected by blue and orange relationship lines.
nodes.creator: Built By Bots

nodes.title: What Is XanaNode?
nodes.summary: XanaNode is a protocol for interoperable knowledge substrates, not merely a graph viewer or static site theme.
nodes.content:
XanaNode is best understood in layers.

The ontology defines what kinds of things can be represented.

The relationship vocabulary defines why those things are connected.

A substrate is an independently authored graph using that vocabulary.

Protocol artifacts make that graph portable: substrate.json identifies the substrate, node records describe addressable objects, relationships.json preserves typed edges, schemas validate the artifacts, and reports explain compatibility or merge decisions.

A renderer, such as this Hugo theme, is one way to publish and navigate a substrate. It is not the protocol itself.

The larger goal is federation: many independently moderated substrates that can still interoperate because they declare their schemas and relationships clearly.

nodes.title: Using Hugo with Protocol JSON
nodes.summary: How to use the Hugo theme as a read-only XanaNode viewer for Markdown-authored nodes, mounted substrates, and imported protocol JSON.
nodes.content:
Hugo is the static publishing layer. It is not the protocol.

Use Markdown when you want prose pages, essays, explainers, sources, or authored trails. Use protocol JSON when you already have validated substrate records from Core, Workspace, another tool, or another substrate.

The import path is intentionally simple. Drop a pack into the site, then let the build compose it with the local substrate:



nodes.json can contain either a single substrate node, an array of substrate nodes, or an object with a nodes array. relationships.json can contain either an array of relationships or an object with a relationships array.

The example site uses two mounted substrates plus one imported JSON layer:

params.xananode.packs in hugo.yaml mounts the protocol minimal substrate from the Core/Protocol submodule. It demonstrates the base model: substrate manifest, node records, typed relationships, evidence, stable fragments, and transclusion.
params.xananode.packs also mounts the sibling XanaNode Canonical Substrate repository so the example can federate with the current canonical vocabulary and project stack.
imports/lineage/ extends that model with the fuller human-computer augmentation linea

nodes.title: Start Here
nodes.summary: The canonical starting essay for the XanaNode example substrate.
nodes.content:
XanaNode begins with a simple claim: civilization is good at publishing information and bad at preserving the relationships that make information understandable.

A page can say something. A graph can show where that statement came from, what supports it, what challenges it, what it depends on, and how it changed.

This substrate explains XanaNode using XanaNode itself. Each page is a node, and the graph around it carries the claims, sources, lineage, and relationships that make the page understandable.

Follow the core trail from the problem of isolated documents, through typed relationships, provenance, transclusion, namespacing, merge validation, and federation.

Then follow the protocol artifacts trail to see the manifest, node records, relationship list, schemas, registries, extension examples, reports, and governance rules that make federation concrete.

If you are building a Hugo site from existing protocol JSON, read Using Hugo with Protocol JSON.

nodes.title: XanaNode
nodes.summary: A protocol for interoperable, independently authored knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments.
nodes.content:
XanaNode is a protocol, ontology, and publishing architecture for interoperable knowledge substrates.

It treats relationships as first-class structures, not decoration around documents. Claims, sources, people, events, media, trails, schemas, fragments, and revisions become addressable nodes connected by typed relationships.

The core schema gives independently authored substrates enough shared meaning to validate, compare, merge, federate, or analyze them without requiring a single central source of truth.

nodes.title: Unlabeled Hyperlink
nodes.summary: A link that connects two resources without preserving why the connection matters.
nodes.content:
The web hyperlink is powerful because it is simple. It spread because it was easy.

Its weakness is that it usually does not say whether the target supports, contradicts, defines, documents, quotes, explains, or merely mentions the source.

nodes.title: Typed Relationships
nodes.summary: Explicit relationship meanings that explain why two nodes are connected.
nodes.content:
A normal hyperlink says that two things are connected.

A typed relationship says how they are connected.

That difference matters. supports, contradicts, explains, derived_from, memorializes, transcludes, and deep_links_to carry different meanings and should not be flattened into generic relatedness.

nodes.title: Tumbler Addressing
nodes.summary: Persistent, location-independent addressing for nodes, relationships, revisions, and fragments inside a substrate.
nodes.content:
Tumbler addressing keeps content addressable when files move, URLs change, or layouts are rebuilt.

XanaNode uses stable node identifiers and stable fragment identifiers for deep linking and transclusion.

The protocol-level address profile uses namespaced ids such as example.minimal:source/as-we-may-think#fragment/0004 so fragments can remain identifiable across renderers and rebuilds.

This page resolves a local stable fragment. The shortcode below points at fragment 0004 inside the as-we-may-think source node:

{{< xana ref="as-we-may-think@v1/0004" label="Tumbler-addressed fragment" >}}

If the source file moves or the page layout changes, the reference still names the logical node, version, and fragment id rather than a fragile screen location.

nodes.title: Transclusion
nodes.summary: Including a specific versioned fragment by reference rather than copying it, preserving identity, attribution, and revision lineage.
nodes.content:
Transclusion includes a source fragment by reference instead of turning it into an anonymous copy.

In this XanaNode substrate, a transclusion points to a stable fragment id inside a versioned source node.

The rendered passage keeps its source identity, selector metadata, attribution path, and protocol tumbler address.

This page includes fragment 0001 from the Xanadu source node:

{{< xana ref="xanadu-document-interconnection@v1/0001" label="Transcluded source fragment" >}}

The block above is backed by xanadu-document-interconnection@v1/0001, which resolves to the protocol address xananode.example:source/xanadu-document-interconnection#fragment/0001.

nodes.title: Substrate Relationship List
nodes.summary: The protocol-level relationship file containing typed edges with stable ids, source and target ids, summaries, visibility, weights, and assertion provenance.
nodes.content:
relationships.json is the edge list for a substrate.

Each relationship has its own id, source, target, type, and summary. It can also carry weight, visibility, assertion provenance, confidence, evidence, federation metadata, and tumbler references.

A relationship list can be authored directly, imported from packs, or derived from structured content such as primary media links, trail membership, and stable transclusion references.

nodes.title: Substrate Node Record
nodes.summary: A JSON representation of a node with a protocol identifier, type, summary, importance, provenance fields, and incoming or outgoing relationship summaries.
nodes.content:
A node record is the machine-readable counterpart to a human-readable page.

It preserves the protocol id, title, type, importance, summary, provenance fields, and incoming or outgoing relationship summaries needed for tools to traverse the substrate without scraping HTML.

Addressable fragments can also be published as protocol fragment node records with source_node, fragment_id, tumbler, and selector metadata.

nodes.title: Substrate Manifest
nodes.summary: The root metadata file that identifies a substrate, its namespace, version, schema version, imports, maintainers, and extension declarations.
nodes.content:
substrate.json is the first file another tool should read.

It identifies the substrate, namespace, substrate version, targeted core schema version, imports, maintainers, and extension declarations.

This theme generates substrate.json during preparation so the human site and machine-readable substrate stay aligned.

nodes.title: Single Source of Truth
nodes.summary: A centralized model where one canonical location or authority controls what is treated as valid.
nodes.content:
Single-source-of-truth systems are useful when consensus, control, and simplicity matter.

They become fragile when knowledge is disputed, partial, culturally specific, politically sensitive, or evolving.

XanaNode favors interoperable sources of context over one enforced source of truth.

nodes.title: Schema Extension
nodes.summary: The ability for a substrate to add custom node or relationship types while remaining compatible with XanaNode Core.
nodes.content:
XanaNode Core is intentionally shared, but it is not the only vocabulary a substrate can use.

The core schema provides shared interoperability. Extension schemas allow local domains to define additional meanings without breaking the whole system.

A custom type is valid when it is namespaced, described, versioned, machine-readable, and preserved even by tools that do not understand it.

The custom extension substrate example shows this in practice: a local domain can define additional types while still publishing the core manifest, node, relationship, and schema files expected by XanaNode tools.

nodes.title: Review Suggestions
nodes.summary: Generated, user-approved suggestions for autolinking node titles, aliases, repeated fragments, and imported substrate changes without rewriting content automatically.

nodes.title: Provenance
nodes.summary: The visible lineage of where information came from, how it changed, who asserted it, and what evidence it depends on.
nodes.content:
Provenance is not decorative metadata. It is part of meaning.

A claim without provenance may still be useful, but its status is different from a claim tied to sources, authorship, revisions, and evidence.

nodes.title: Protocol Artifacts
nodes.summary: The machine-readable files and reports that let a XanaNode substrate be validated, compared, merged, and federated.
nodes.content:
The protocol is not only a set of ideas. It is a set of publishable artifacts.

A published substrate can expose those artifacts as static JSON: substrate.json, relationships.json, one JSON record per node, schema files, fragment data, and pending review suggestions.

Human pages make the substrate readable. Protocol artifacts make it inspectable, validatable, comparable, and federatable by other tools.

nodes.title: Namespacing
nodes.summary: A way to identify which substrate or schema owns a node type, relationship type, node, or fragment.
nodes.content:
Namespacing prevents independent substrates and extension vocabularies from colliding.

The core namespace is xananode. A substrate can use its own namespace for local nodes and extension types, such as example.museum:artifact or example.researcher_a:concept/knowledge-substrate.

Unknown namespaced types are not invalid knowledge. They are declared extensions that can be displayed, preserved, mapped, or quarantined until another system understands them.

nodes.title: Namespace Registry
nodes.summary: A registry of known namespace identifiers used to avoid collisions and help tools recognize who owns a substrate, schema, or extension vocabulary.
nodes.content:
The namespace registry records known namespace ids and their owners.

It does not make every other namespace invalid. It helps tools distinguish recognized namespaces from private, experimental, or not-yet-registered ones while still preserving unknown data.

nodes.title: Moderation Model
nodes.summary: The governance model that lets each substrate decide its own trust, inclusion, review, and publication rules.
nodes.content:
XanaNode does not require one global moderation authority.

Each substrate can decide what it publishes, imports, hides, rejects, or marks as disputed. Federation works because those decisions are visible rather than silently normalized away.

nodes.title: Merge Validation
nodes.summary: The process of safely combining or comparing substrates without destroying authorship, provenance, disagreement, or local moderation.
nodes.content:
Merge validation is not deduplication.

A merge-safe system does not automatically collapse two nodes with similar titles into one truth. It compares identity, provenance, source lineage, schema compatibility, and relationship meaning.

The goal is to preserve both substrates while making their overlaps and differences navigable.

Compatibility reports explain whether two substrates can be compared safely. Merge reports preserve the proposed mappings, conflicts, unresolved issues, and identity decisions produced by that comparison.

nodes.title: Merge Report
nodes.summary: A report that explains proposed mappings, conflicts, identity decisions, preserved disagreements, and unresolved issues when substrates are compared or merged.
nodes.content:
A merge report is not a command to flatten two substrates into one truth.

It is an audit trail for a comparison. It preserves proposed identity matches, conflicts, unresolved decisions, warnings, and the reasoning behind mappings so disagreement remains navigable after analysis.

The canonical federation example uses a merge report to keep two overlapping concepts distinct while still recording their possible relationship.

nodes.title: Known Implementations Registry
nodes.summary: A registry of tools, themes, validators, renderers, and substrates that claim some level of XanaNode compatibility.
nodes.content:
The known implementations registry is a discovery aid for the ecosystem.

It lists tools, themes, validators, renderers, and example substrates that claim a level of XanaNode compatibility.

The registry does not replace validation. It gives implementers real targets to inspect, compare, and test against.

nodes.title: Knowledge Substrate
nodes.summary: The structure that determines how information relates, persists, evolves, and can be navigated as knowledge.
nodes.content:
A knowledge substrate is not the information itself. It is the structure that lets information remain understandable across time.

The same facts arranged as isolated documents, unlabeled hyperlinks, or typed relationships produce different knowledge capabilities. XanaNode makes that structure explicit.

nodes.title: Independent Moderation
nodes.summary: The principle that each substrate may govern its own content while still remaining interoperable with others.
nodes.content:
Independent moderation means each substrate controls what it accepts, rejects, disputes, hides, deprecates, or marks as uncertain.

Federation does not require one moderation team to control validity for everyone. Local decisions remain visible as provenance, confidence, claims, counterclaims, evidence, and mappings across substrates.

nodes.title: Fragment Node
nodes.summary: A node representing a stable addressable portion of another source, publication, media object, or node.
nodes.content:
Fragment nodes represent stable addressable parts of larger objects.

Instead of pointing only to a whole source, a fragment carries its own id, selector metadata, tumbler address, and relationship history.

For example, this concept cites a specific fragment of the Mother of All Demos source node:

{{< xana ref="mother-of-all-demos@v1/0001" label="Fragment node example" >}}

That fragment is not copied into this page. It is rendered from the source node's authored stable fragment map.

nodes.title: Federation Rules
nodes.summary: The rules for exchanging, comparing, and preserving independently governed substrates without collapsing them into one authority.
nodes.content:
Federation starts from substrate sovereignty.

Another substrate may import, map, compare, or reject data, but it must not silently overwrite provenance, collapse disagreement, or erase local moderation decisions.

The canonical rule is simple: merge by adding structure, not by destroying context.

nodes.title: Federation Example
nodes.summary: A protocol example showing two independently authored substrates being compared through compatibility and merge reports.
nodes.content:
The canonical federation example makes the protocol concrete.

It compares two independently authored substrates: one models a knowledge substrate, the other models a memory substrate. Each keeps its own namespace and local records.

The compatibility report records how much interoperability is safe. The merge report records mappings and the decision not to collapse overlapping concepts into one forced truth.

nodes.title: Federated Knowledge Substrates
nodes.summary: Independently authored XanaNode substrates that can interoperate, merge, compare, and preserve disagreement without a central authority.
nodes.content:
Federation connects independently authored substrates without making them surrender ownership.

Each person, lab, community, archive, or institution keeps its own namespace, governance, and provenance. Those substrates do not need to agree. They need to describe nodes, relationships, schemas, and provenance clearly enough that another tool can ingest, map, compare, or ignore them safely.

Federation turns isolated knowledge graphs into comparable civilizational memory.

In protocol terms, federation depends on manifests, namespaces, compatibility reports, merge reports, and governance rules that prevent one substrate from silently overwriting another.

nodes.title: Dispute Resolution
nodes.summary: A governance process for preserving contested claims, identity matches, relationships, and moderation decisions without pretending disagreement has disappeared.
nodes.content:
Disputes are knowledge, not cleanup errors.

XanaNode preserves disagreement about claims, relationships, identity mappings, and merge decisions. The contested state remains traceable through provenance, evidence, counterevidence, and explicit relationship types such as contradicts, disputes, possible_match, and disputed_identity.

The goal is navigable uncertainty, not instant consensus.

nodes.title: Deep Linking
nodes.summary: Pointing to a specific addressable fragment rather than only to a whole document or node.
nodes.content:
Deep linking points at an addressable part of a node rather than only at a whole page.

In XanaNode, stable fragment ids and tumbler addresses give those links protocol identity. A deep link can identify the source node, version, and fragment being discussed or transcluded.

That is the practical bridge between ordinary hyperlinks and the provenance-preserving reuse imagined by Xanadu.

nodes.title: Custom Extension Substrate
nodes.summary: An example substrate that defines namespaced domain-specific node and relationship types while preserving compatibility with XanaNode Core.
nodes.content:
The custom extension substrate example shows local vocabulary coexisting with XanaNode Core.

The canonical example defines a museum namespace with a custom artifact node type and custom curatorial relationship types.

Tools that do not understand those custom types still preserve them as declared, namespaced, versioned extensions instead of discarding them.

nodes.title: Compatibility Report
nodes.summary: A report that records how one substrate or tool understands another substrate, including compatibility level, supported schemas, warnings, and blocking issues.
nodes.content:
A compatibility report records how one implementation understands a substrate.

It names the compatibility level reached, the schema versions recognized, unsupported extensions, warnings, and any blocking issues.

The canonical protocol example publishes a compatibility report for its federation example, and this Hugo implementation publishes the schema needed to validate that report shape.

nodes.title: Compatibility Levels
nodes.summary: A staged way to describe how much of a substrate another implementation can recognize, validate, extend, merge, or federate.
nodes.content:
Compatibility is not all-or-nothing.

The protocol describes six practical levels: opaque storage, manifest recognition, core validation, declared extensions, merge candidacy, and federation readiness.

The point is preservation. A tool can keep data it does not fully understand, report the compatibility level it reached, and avoid pretending that unknown extensions are invalid knowledge.

nodes.title: Civilizational Memory
nodes.summary: A long-term, federated memory layer where knowledge remains traceable, disputed, interoperable, and navigable across generations.
nodes.content:
Civilizational memory is the long-term horizon for XanaNode.

It is not one website, database, or moderation team. It is a network of independently authored substrates that preserve lineage, disagreement, evidence, and context while remaining interoperable.

The protocol pieces in this example are deliberately small, but they point at that larger pattern: locally owned knowledge that can still be compared, mapped, cited, and preserved across systems.

nodes.title: Canonical Type Policy
nodes.summary: The governance rule that separates shared core node and relationship types from namespaced extension types.
nodes.content:
Canonical types are the shared vocabulary that let independent substrates understand one another.

The core stays small, stable, and broadly useful. Domain-specific meanings belong in namespaced extension schemas unless they become common enough to justify canonical status.

This keeps the core interoperable while still letting local substrates model museums, legal records, biology, archives, or other specialized domains precisely.

nodes.title: A knowledge substrate is structure, not information
nodes.summary: A knowledge substrate is not the information itself; it is the structure that determines how information relates, persists, and can be understood.
nodes.content:
Most information systems focus on what is stored.

XanaNode focuses on how what is stored relates.

nodes.title: Typed relationships preserve context that hyperlinks lose
nodes.summary: Typed relationships preserve why nodes are connected, while ordinary hyperlinks usually preserve only that a connection exists.
nodes.content:
A hyperlink can connect a source to a target without saying whether the source cites, defines, contradicts, explains, quotes, or merely mentions the target.

Typed relationships make that missing meaning explicit.

nodes.title: Substrates should merge without collapsing disagreement
nodes.summary: Federated substrates should preserve authorship, provenance, and disagreement rather than forcing one merged source of truth.
nodes.content:
The goal of merging substrates is not to erase differences.

The goal is to expose correspondences, conflicts, overlaps, and gaps while preserving who asserted what and why.

nodes.title: Implementations Identify XanaNode Compatibility
nodes.summary: Implementations should identify themselves as XanaNode-compatible and reference the canonical specification without implying official ownership.
nodes.content: Implementations should identify themselves as XanaNode-compatible and reference the canonical specification without implying official ownership.

nodes.title: Protocol Docs Are Licensed CC-BY 4.0
nodes.summary: XanaNode protocol documentation is licensed under CC-BY 4.0 so the docs can be shared and adapted with attribution.
nodes.content: XanaNode protocol documentation is licensed under CC-BY 4.0 so the docs can be shared and adapted with attribution.

nodes.title: Relationships Preserve Knowledge
nodes.summary: Knowledge survives movement between tools when relationships carry meaning, evidence, authorship, and addressable context instead of collapsing into anonymous links.
nodes.content: Knowledge survives movement between tools when relationships carry meaning, evidence, authorship, and addressable context instead of collapsing into anonymous links.

nodes.title: Schemas, Validators, And Reference Code Are Apache-2.0
nodes.summary: XanaNode schemas, validators, tooling, and reference implementation code are licensed under Apache-2.0 for broad adoption.
nodes.content: XanaNode schemas, validators, tooling, and reference implementation code are licensed under Apache-2.0 for broad adoption.

nodes.title: XanaNode Name And Logo Are Trademarked
nodes.summary: The XanaNode name, logo, and related brand assets are governed as trademarks even when protocol docs and code are openly licensed.
nodes.content: The XanaNode name, logo, and related brand assets are governed as trademarks even when protocol docs and code are openly licensed.

nodes.title: XanaNode Contributors
nodes.summary: The people and future maintainers who author, review, preserve, and project XanaNode-compatible substrates.
nodes.content: The people and future maintainers who author, review, preserve, and project XanaNode-compatible substrates.

nodes.title: Attribution License
nodes.summary: A license pattern that allows reuse while requiring credit to the original author or project.
nodes.content: A license pattern that allows reuse while requiring credit to the original author or project.

nodes.title: Compatibility Attribution
nodes.summary: The convention that implementations should identify themselves as XanaNode-compatible and point to the canonical specification without implying ownership or endorsement.
nodes.content: The convention that implementations should identify themselves as XanaNode-compatible and point to the canonical specification without implying ownership or endorsement.

nodes.title: Content License
nodes.summary: A license applied to authored text, documentation, examples, or explanatory material rather than executable reference code.
nodes.content: A license applied to authored text, documentation, examples, or explanatory material rather than executable reference code.

nodes.title: Knowledge Substrate
nodes.summary: The graph-shaped structure that lets information remain understandable across time by making claims, sources, media, fragments, and relationships explicit.
nodes.content: The graph-shaped structure that lets information remain understandable across time by making claims, sources, media, fragments, and relationships explicit.

nodes.title: Licensing
nodes.summary: The legal layer that describes how documents, schemas, code, media, and names may be copied, reused, attributed, modified, or governed.
nodes.content: The legal layer that describes how documents, schemas, code, media, and names may be copied, reused, attributed, modified, or governed.

nodes.title: Open License
nodes.summary: A license that grants broad public permission to use, share, adapt, or implement a work while preserving stated conditions such as attribution or notices.
nodes.content: A license that grants broad public permission to use, share, adapt, or implement a work while preserving stated conditions such as attribution or notices.

nodes.title: Permissive Open Source License
nodes.summary: An open source license family that allows broad reuse, modification, distribution, and commercial adoption with limited obligations.
nodes.content: An open source license family that allows broad reuse, modification, distribution, and commercial adoption with limited obligations.

nodes.title: Protocol Artifacts
nodes.summary: The portable JSON files, schemas, fragments, reports, and media references that allow tools to validate, compare, mount, import, merge, and render substrates.
nodes.content: The portable JSON files, schemas, fragments, reports, and media references that allow tools to validate, compare, mount, import, merge, and render substrates.

nodes.title: Software License
nodes.summary: A license applied to source code, validators, tooling, schemas, and implementation artifacts.
nodes.content: A license applied to source code, validators, tooling, schemas, and implementation artifacts.

nodes.title: Substrate Projection Layer
nodes.summary: A renderer or lens that presents a substrate for a particular use without becoming the substrate itself.
nodes.content: A renderer or lens that presents a substrate for a particular use without becoming the substrate itself.

nodes.title: Trademark
nodes.summary: A protected name, logo, or mark that identifies the source and reputation of a project, product, organization, or community.
nodes.content: A protected name, logo, or mark that identifies the source and reputation of a project, product, organization, or community.

nodes.title: XanaNode
nodes.summary: A protocol for independently authored knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments.
nodes.content: A protocol for independently authored knowledge substrates that preserve relationships, provenance, lineage, disagreement, and addressable fragments.

nodes.title: What Is XanaNode?
nodes.summary: An explanation of XanaNode as a layered protocol, not a single viewer, website, repository, or graph visualization.
nodes.content: An explanation of XanaNode as a layered protocol, not a single viewer, website, repository, or graph visualization.

nodes.title: Markdown Recreation Friction
nodes.summary: Forcing authors to recreate validated protocol nodes as Markdown creates duplicate governance surfaces and loses the benefit of portable substrate artifacts.
nodes.content: Forcing authors to recreate validated protocol nodes as Markdown creates duplicate governance surfaces and loses the benefit of portable substrate artifacts.

nodes.content: Electron authoring workbench for creating nodes, editing protocol metadata, navigating graph context, and previewing projection layers.

nodes.title: XanaNode Workspace
nodes.summary: Local-first workspace engine for creating, opening, validating, building, importing, and snapshotting XanaNode substrates.
nodes.content: Local-first workspace engine for creating, opening, validating, building, importing, and snapshotting XanaNode substrates.

nodes.title: XNP-0004 Merge Validation
nodes.summary: A protocol proposal describing merge validation for independently authored substrates.
nodes.content: A protocol proposal describing merge validation for independently authored substrates.

nodes.title: How do I use XanaNode myself?
nodes.summary: Start with a substrate, add nodes and typed relationships, validate it with Core, and project it with a renderer such as Hugo or Studio.
nodes.content: A user can begin with a small question, claim, source, or trail. Core can initialize a substrate, validate nodes and relationships, generate review suggestions, and build packs. Hugo can mount or import those packs for a public website. Studio can open a pack as a local working copy so authors can edit without pretending they own someone else's substrate.

nodes.title: How do sharing rules work?
nodes.summary: Nodes are shareable by default, but a substrate can exclude individual nodes, relationships, trails, or selected groups from shared exports.
nodes.content: XanaNode assumes portability unless an author says otherwise. A private workspace can hold a large substrate while its pack export omits sensitive records. Sharing rules can live on the substrate manifest or on individual nodes, and official tools must respect those rules before publishing, exporting, mounting, or federating records.

nodes.title: Is XanaNode a real working stack?
nodes.summary: Yes. XanaNode has a protocol, Core SDK, Hugo projection, Workspace layer, and Studio authoring application that work together.
nodes.content: XanaNode is not only a design idea. The current stack includes protocol schemas and governance rules, a Core SDK for validation and pack building, Hugo for static public projection, Workspace for local substrate handling, and Studio for desktop authoring. The pieces are versioned so a reader can trace what each part does and why it exists.

nodes.title: Who made XanaNode?
nodes.summary: XanaNode is authored by Christian Siefen. Built By Bots remains part of the project's historical lineage, but XanaNode.com is the long-term public home of the work.
nodes.content: The canonical project substrate identifies Christian Siefen as the human author of XanaNode. It also preserves historical lineage records, including the temporary Built By Bots bridge period, the current XanaNode.com domain, the public repositories, branding, and support links. That provenance matters because people should be able to trace who authored a substrate, how the public identity evolved, and where project work now lives.

nodes.title: Why does XanaNode exist?
nodes.summary: XanaNode exists to preserve relationships, provenance, disagreement, lineage, and reusable fragments as durable knowledge structure.
nodes.content: A normal website can publish text. XanaNode publishes the relationships behind the text. It is meant for authored knowledge substrates where sources, claims, trails, versions, transclusions, conflicts, and projection layers remain inspectable instead of disappearing into a flat page.

nodes.title: Claim
nodes.summary: A specific assertion that can be supported, disputed, revised, deprecated, or rejected.
nodes.content: A specific assertion that can be supported, disputed, revised, deprecated, or rejected.

nodes.title: Communication
nodes.summary: A first-class communication event or artifact whose topology, directionality, participants, and pattern affect how knowledge moved.
nodes.content: A first-class communication event or artifact whose topology, directionality, participants, and pattern affect how knowledge moved.

nodes.title: Community
nodes.summary: A group organized around an identity, platform, shared work, practice, interest, or tradition.
nodes.content: A group organized around an identity, platform, shared work, practice, interest, or tradition.

nodes.title: Concept
nodes.summary: An idea, pattern, theory, failure mode, or explanatory structure. A concept is built from claims.
nodes.content: An idea, pattern, theory, failure mode, or explanatory structure. A concept is built from claims.

nodes.title: Essay
nodes.summary: A human-authored argument that connects claims, sources, observations, and concepts.
nodes.content: A human-authored argument that connects claims, sources, observations, and concepts.

nodes.title: Event
nodes.summary: Something that happened at a time and optionally at a place.
nodes.content: Something that happened at a time and optionally at a place.

nodes.title: Fragment
nodes.summary: A stable, addressable portion of another node, source, media object, or publication used for deep linking, quotation, citation, transclusion, and revision-aware reference.
nodes.content: A stable, addressable portion of another node, source, media object, or publication used for deep linking, quotation, citation, transclusion, and revision-aware reference.

nodes.title: Hypothesis
nodes.summary: A provisional explanation, prediction, or model that can be tested, supported, falsified, revised, or abandoned.
nodes.content: A provisional explanation, prediction, or model that can be tested, supported, falsified, revised, or abandoned.

nodes.title: Item
nodes.summary: A discrete physical thing, product, ingredient, component, tool, or other tangible object that can be counted, assembled, consumed, used, or stored.
nodes.content: A discrete physical thing, product, ingredient, component, tool, or other tangible object that can be counted, assembled, consumed, used, or stored.

nodes.title: Knowledge Gap
nodes.summary: A known absence, uncertainty, missing source, missing citation, lost document, unknown author, or unresolved area in a substrate.
nodes.content: A known absence, uncertainty, missing source, missing citation, lost document, unknown author, or unresolved area in a substrate.

nodes.title: Media
nodes.summary: An image, video, audio clip, screenshot, diagram, document, or other media item with provenance.
nodes.content: An image, video, audio clip, screenshot, diagram, document, or other media item with provenance.

nodes.title: Observation

nodes.content: A real-world case, symptom, example, incident, failure, or pattern being analyzed.

nodes.title: Organization

nodes.content: A company, institution, agency, lab, school, collective, standards body, nonprofit, or group.

nodes.title: Person

nodes.content: A human actor who created, influenced, witnessed, argued, documented, or participated in something.

nodes.title: Place

nodes.content: A physical or virtual location where people, projects, organizations, or events exist or occurred.

nodes.title: Problem

nodes.content: A difficulty, failure mode, design challenge, risk, or open need that organizes inquiry and work.

nodes.title: Project

nodes.content: A built, proposed, fictional, abandoned, or active system with a purpose and lineage.

nodes.title: Publication

nodes.content: A released intellectual or creative work such as a book, article, paper, film, episode, or talk.

nodes.title: Question

nodes.content: An explicit inquiry, prompt, research question, or unresolved ask that motivates investigation and can be answered, partially answered, disputed, or left open.

{
  "id": "example.merge:report/federation-example-001",
  "status": "review-needed",
  "generated_at": "2026-01-01T00:00:00Z",
  "substrates": [
    "example.researcher_a",
    "example.researcher_b"
  ],
  "compatibility_level": 4,
  "mappings": [
    {
      "id": "example.merge:mapping/knowledge-memory-substrate",
      "source": "example.researcher_a:concept/knowledge-substrate",
      "target": "example.researcher_b:concept/memory-substrate",
      "type": "possible_match",
      "confidence": 0.78,
      "status": "review-needed",
      "preserves_local_nodes": true,
      "creates_relationships": [
        "example.researcher_a:rel/knowledge-substrate-possible-match-memory-substrate",
        "example.researcher_b:rel/memory-substrate-possible-match-knowledge-substrate"
      ],
      "rationale": "Both nodes describe substrate-like structures for preserving context and relationships, but one emphasizes knowledge objects while the other emphasizes memory over time."
    }
  ],
  "conflicts": [
    {
      "id": "example.merge:conflict/do-not-collapse-concepts",
      "type": "identity_ambiguity",
      "nodes": [
        "example.researcher_a:concept/knowledge-substrate",
        "example.researcher_b:concept/memory-substrate"
      ],
      "resolution": "preserve_both",
      "summary": "The merge pass records a possible match but does not replace either local concept with a canonical node."
    }
  ],
  "warnings": [
    "The two concepts are close but should not be automatically collapsed into a single canonical node."
  ]
}

{
  "id": "example.minimal",
  "name": "Minimal XanaNode Substrate",
  "description": "A tiny but complete XanaNode substrate showing that every meaningful node is connected through typed relationships, not only by a trail.",
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "namespace": "example.minimal",
  "repository": {
    "type": "git",
    "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/xananode.git",
    "default_branch": "main",
    "path": "examples/minimal-substrate"
  },
  "federation": {
    "feed": "https://example.com/xananode/minimal/feed.json",
    "merge_reports": "https://example.com/xananode/minimal/merge-reports/"
  },
  "trust": {
    "signed": false,
    "trust_scope": [
      "local_substrate",
      "assertion"
    ]
  },
  "schema_version": "xananode-core@0.5.0",
  "license": "CC0-1.0",
  "branding": {
    "brand_name": "XanaNode",
    "tagline": "Relationships preserve knowledge",
    "description": "Canonical XanaNode protocol branding used by protocol examples and downstream project tools.",
    "primary_media": "example.minimal:media/xananode-icon",
    "logo_media": "example.minimal:media/xananode-icon",
    "icon_media": "example.minimal:media/xananode-icon",
    "primary_color": "#55D6BE",
    "accent_color": "#FF8C00",
    "background_color": "#05070A",
    "text_color": "#F8FAFC",
    "source_node": "example.minimal:media/xananode-icon",
    "rights_status": "open-source",
    "license": "MIT",
    "updated_at": "2026-06-19T00:00:00Z",
    "reason": "Protocol branding is modeled as substrate metadata and media so changes to project identity can be preserved as knowledge."
  },
  "maintainers": [
    {
      "name": "Example Maintainer",
      "url": "https://example.com"
    }
  ],
  "imports": [
    "xananode:core"
  ]
}

    {
      "id": "xananode.canonical",
      "name": "XanaNode Canonical Substrate",
      "namespace": "xananode.canonical",
      "description": "The canonical XanaNode substrate for protocol vocabulary, project identity, governance, software stack records, raw source snapshots, branding, and self-referential examples.",
      "status": "canonical",
      "repository": {
        "type": "git",
        "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Canonical-Substrate.git",
        "default_branch": "main"
      },
      "recommended_mode": "mounted",
      "licenses": ["CC-BY-4.0", "Apache-2.0"],
      "homepage": "https://xananode.com/"
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.alphabet",
      "name": "XanaNode Alphabet Substrate",
      "namespace": "xananode.alphabet",
      "description": "A deliberately small example substrate with a start trail, A-Z letter nodes, typed relationships, and bundled media assets for every letter.",
      "status": "example",
      "repository": {
        "type": "git",
        "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Alphabet-Substrate.git",
        "default_branch": "main"
      },
      "recommended_mode": "mounted",
      "licenses": ["CC0"],
      "homepage": "https://xananode.com/"
    },
    {
      "id": "xananode.lineage",
      "name": "XanaNode Lost Lineage Substrate",
      "namespace": "xananode.lineage",
      "description": "A lineage substrate for the historical and conceptual path into XanaNode.",
      "status": "community",
      "repository": {
        "type": "git",

      "homepage": "https://xananode.com/"

}

{
  "implementations": [
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Protocol",

      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode",
      "protocol_role": "schema-and-governance-source",
      "description": "Canonical XanaNode protocol repository containing schemas, registries, specs, governance, examples, branding, and proposals.",
      "consumes": [],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/",
        "../registry/",
        "../specs/",
        "../governance/",
        "../examples/minimal-substrate/"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Core SDK",
      "type": "core-sdk",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Core-SDK",
      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode-Core-SDK",
      "protocol_role": "reference-core-engine",
      "description": "Renderer-independent parser, validator, graph builder, fragment engine, pack composer, suggestion engine, and exporter for XanaNode substrates.",
      "consumes": [
        "protocol schemas",
        "node files",
        "relationship files",
        "substrate manifests",
        "substrate packs"
      ],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/substrate-manifest.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-node.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-relationships.schema.json",
        "../schemas/xananode-node-types.v0.3.0.json",
        "../schemas/xananode-relationship-types.v0.5.0.json"

    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Hugo Theme",
      "type": "static-site-reference-implementation",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/xananode-hugo",
      "repository": "kingc95/xananode-hugo",
      "protocol_role": "reference-renderer",
      "description": "Static-site renderer and graph viewer for XanaNode-compatible substrates.",
      "consumes": [
        "substrate manifests",
        "node files",
        "relationship files",
        "schema registries"
      ],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/substrate-manifest.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-node.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-relationships.schema.json",
        "../examples/minimal-substrate/"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Workspace",
      "type": "workspace-engine",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Workspace",
      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode-Workspace",
      "protocol_role": "local-first-workspace-layer",
      "description": "Local-first workspace engine for creating, opening, validating, building, importing, and snapshotting XanaNode substrates.",
      "consumes": [
        "Core SDK APIs",
        "substrate manifests",
        "node files",
        "relationship files",
        "author profiles"
      ],
      "related_protocol_artifacts": [
        "../schemas/author-profile.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-manifest.schema.json",
        "../schemas/substrate-node.schema.json"
      ]
    },
    {
      "name": "XanaNode Studio",
      "type": "desktop-authoring-application",
      "status": "active",
      "url": "https://github.com/kingc95/XanaNode-Studio",
      "repository": "kingc95/XanaNode-Studio",
      "protocol_role": "desktop-authoring-interface",
      "description": "Electron authoring workbench for creating nodes, editing protocol metadata, navigating graph context, and previewing projection layers.",
      "consumes": [
        "Workspace APIs",
        "Core SDK APIs",
        "protocol registries",
        "protocol branding"

}

{
  "version": "0.5.0",
  "relationship_types": [
    {
      "type": "abstraction_of",
      "label": "Abstraction of",
      "category": "cognition",
      "meaning": "The source abstracts away details from the target while preserving a relevant structure.",
      "inverse": "concrete_example_of",
      "default_weight": 4,
      "default_visibility": "primary",
      "namespace": "xananode",
      "id": "xananode:abstraction_of",
      "core": true,
      "extends": null,
      "color": "#7c3aed",
      "inverse_color": "#ddd6fe",
      "line_style": "solid",
      "inverse_line_style": "dashed",

nodes.title: Schemas / Xananode Substrate Layout.V0.1.0
nodes.summary: schemas/xananode-substrate-layout.v0.1.0.json is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
{
  "$schema": "./substrate-layout.schema.json",
  "id": "xananode.substrate-layout",
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "description": "The standard directory layout for a portable XanaNode substrate root.",
  "root_contract": "A substrate root is the directory containing substrate.json. Tools should be able to validate, pack, mount, and project a substrate from this root without needing private application state.",
  "required_paths": [
    {
      "path": "substrate.json",
      "role": "substrate_manifest",
      "required": true,
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "The substrate manifest declaring id, name, namespace, version, repository, imports, sharing policy, and build/provenance metadata.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    }
  ],
  "recognized_paths": [
    {
      "path": "nodes/*.json",
      "role": "protocol_nodes",
      "kind": "glob",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Canonical protocol node records. A hand-built substrate may use this directory directly.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "nodes.json",
      "role": "protocol_node_bundle",
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "An alternate bundled protocol node artifact with a top-level nodes array.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "relationships.json",
      "role": "protocol_relationships",
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Typed relationship records for the substrate.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "assets/**",
      "role": "portable_media_and_source_assets",
      "kind": "glob",
      "description": "Images, PDFs, audio, video, source snapshots, projection media, documents, and other files carried by the substrate.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "schemas/*.json",
      "role": "local_schema_extensions",
      "kind": "glob",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Namespaced node type, relationship type, property, layout, or validation extensions.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "reports/*.json",
      "role": "reports",
      "kind": "glob",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Merge, compatibility, health, validation, duplicate-file, and intake reports.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "packs/*",
      "role": "mounted_substrate_packs",
      "kind": "glob",
      "description": "Unpacked substrate packs mounted into this substrate for local federation.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "imports/*",
      "role": "incoming_substrate_material",
      "kind": "glob",
      "description": "Dropped-in incoming packs or source material awaiting review, mounting, importing, or merge.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "author-profile.json",
      "role": "default_author_profile",
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Portable default author profile for hand-built or minimal substrates.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": "ro-crate-metadata.json",
      "role": "research_object_metadata",
      "kind": "file",
      "format": "json",
      "description": "Optional RO-Crate metadata describing the substrate as a research object.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    }
  ],
  "authoring_paths": [
    {
      "path": "content/nodes/*.md",
      "role": "markdown_authoring_nodes",
      "kind": "glob",
      "format": "markdown_frontmatter",
      "description": "Markdown authoring projection used by Core, Workspace, Studio, and Hugo workflows. Tools may generate protocol nodes from these files.",
      "tools_must_preserve": true
    },
    {
      "path": ".xananode/**",
      "role": "workspace_metadata",
      "kind": "glob",
      "description": "Local workspace metadata such as preview settings, author lists, caches, and opened-source copies. Public exports may omit private caches but must preserve metadata needed for authored workspace round trips.",
      "tools_must_preserve": false
    }
  ],
  "archive_rules": [
    "A .substrate archive should contain every recognized path unless sharing policy excludes it.",
    "Tools must not include .git, node_modules, public renderer output, transient caches, or generated build directories unless explicitly requested.",
    "Every local media/source file referenced by asset_path, file, source_snapshot, primary media resolution, or registry projection metadata must be included or reported as missing.",
    "Duplicate files should be reported by content hash rather than silently duplicated without provenance.",
    "A hand-built substrate that follows this layout should open in Core, Workspace, Studio, Hugo, or any compatible projection without private tool state."
  ]
}

nodes.content:
# Nodes

This directory contains both canonical XanaNode nodes and local extension nodes.

The file `memex-illustration.json` uses the custom node type:

```text
example.museum:artifact
```

Because the type is namespaced and declared in `schemas/`, other substrates can preserve it even when they do not natively support museum artifacts.

nodes.title: Examples / Custom Extension Substrate / README
nodes.summary: examples/custom-extension-substrate/README.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Custom Extension Substrate Example

This example demonstrates how a substrate can extend XanaNode Core without breaking interoperability.

The substrate defines a local namespace:

```text
example.museum
```

It adds:

- a custom node type: `example.museum:artifact`
- a custom relationship type: `example.museum:curatorial_context_for`

A reader that does not understand these custom types should still preserve them as declared, namespaced, versioned schema extensions. A reader that does understand them can validate and render them more precisely.

nodes.summary: examples/custom-extension-substrate/schemas/README.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Custom Schemas

These schema registry files define local extensions to XanaNode Core.

Custom types should be:

- namespaced
- described in human-readable language
- versioned
- mappable to a core type when possible
- safe to preserve even when another implementation does not fully understand them

nodes.title: Examples / Federation Example / README
nodes.summary: examples/federation-example/README.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Federation Example

This example shows how two independently authored substrates can be compared without forcing a single source of truth.

- `substrate-a/` models "knowledge substrate" as a concept.
- `substrate-b/` models "memory substrate" as a similar concept.
- each substrate records an outbound `possible_match` relationship to the other substrate's local concept.
- `merge-report.json` records the result of a federation/merge pass, including the decision not to collapse the two concepts.
- `compatibility-report.json` records the practical compatibility level reached by the example.

The merge report does not overwrite either substrate. It creates mapping claims and compatibility notes so the two graphs can be analyzed together.

nodes.content:
# Nodes

Each file in this directory is a node.

Every node in this example includes relationship references. This is intentional: the example should demonstrate a connected substrate, not just isolated nodes plus a trail.

The canonical relationship list lives in `../relationships.json`.

nodes.title: Examples / Minimal Substrate / README
nodes.summary: examples/minimal-substrate/README.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Minimal XanaNode Substrate Example

This is the smallest useful XanaNode substrate that still follows the core rule:

> Nodes should not float as isolated facts. Concepts are built from claims, claims are supported by sources, and trails are curated routes through an already-connected graph.

This example uses only canonical XanaNode Core node types:

- `source`
- `concept`
- `claim`
- `person`
- `essay`
- `fragment`
- `trail`

It also uses canonical relationship types:

- `supports`
- `discusses`
- `related_to`
- `defines`
- `has_claim`
- `starts_with`
- `continues_to`
- `features`
- `created`
- `derived_from`
- `transcludes`

## What this example demonstrates

The graph is intentionally tiny, but it is not just a bag of nodes.

```text
As We May Think
  supports
    A knowledge substrate is structure, not just information
      defines
        Knowledge Substrate

As We May Think
  discusses
    Associative Trails
      related_to
        Knowledge Substrate

Start Here
  starts_with
    As We May Think

Relationship Navigation Intro
  transcludes
    As We May Think Fragment 0004
```

## Why the relationships matter

A normal hyperlink can show that the source, claim, and concept are somehow related.

XanaNode requires the relationship to say **how** they are related:

- The source `supports` the claim.
- The claim `defines` the concept.
- The concept `has_claim` so it is not unsupported.
- The source `discusses` associative trails.
- Associative trails are `related_to` the knowledge substrate as historical and conceptual context.
- The trail provides a human reading route, but it is not the only connection.
- The essay `transcludes` a fragment using the versioned tumbler address `example.minimal:source/as-we-may-think@git:8e47e70:examples/minimal-substrate/nodes/source-as-we-may-think.json#fragment/0004@sha256:example-as-we-may-think-fragment-0004`.

## Files

- `substrate.json` declares the substrate namespace and schema version.
- `relationships.json` declares canonical typed edges.
- `nodes/*.json` are individually addressable nodes with relationship references.

nodes.title: Examples / README
nodes.summary: examples/README.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# XanaNode Examples

These examples are protocol examples, not full applications.

They show how a substrate can declare a namespace, define nodes, create typed relationships, extend the core schema, and produce a federation merge report.

## Examples

- `minimal-substrate/` — the smallest useful XanaNode substrate.
- `custom-extension-substrate/` — a substrate that adds custom namespace-specific types.
- `federation-example/` — two independently authored substrates plus a merge report.

The examples intentionally use JSON files instead of a specific CMS, static-site generator, database, or graph renderer. Implementations may store the same structures in Markdown front matter, a database, RDF, graph stores, or other formats.

nodes.title: Governance / Canonical Type Policy
nodes.summary: governance/canonical-type-policy.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Canonical Type Policy

Canonical types are the shared vocabulary that make independent XanaNode substrates interoperable.

A type should become canonical only when it is:

- broadly useful across many domains
- clearly distinguishable from existing types
- describable in plain language
- stable enough to validate
- useful to both humans and machines

Avoid adding canonical types for every domain-specific object. Prefer subtypes or namespaced extensions.

## Projection Style Governance

Canonical relationship types include visual projection metadata. Every canonical relationship type must define:

- `color`
- `inverse_color`
- `line_style`
- `inverse_line_style`

These values are part of the shared vocabulary. Renderers use them to stratify connective lines so relationship meaning remains visible in graph projections, trails, maps, and other views.

Implementations may adapt the exact rendered appearance for accessibility, contrast, print, color-blind themes, or device constraints, but they should not collapse all relationship types into one undifferentiated line treatment while claiming full protocol projection compliance.

## Multi-Role Node Projection

The primary node `type` remains the authoritative validation and routing category. Secondary roles belong in `facets`, and narrower labels belong in `subtype` or `subtypes`.

Projection layers should preserve visible evidence of every recognized role. When a node has facets that match known node types, the projected mark should mix the primary type color with the facet type colors. Slices, rings, bands, layered borders, or another accessible mixed treatment are all valid. The important governance rule is that the projection must not silently flatten a multi-role node into one color when the substrate says the node participates in several modeling categories.

Schema vocabulary terms, schema fields, property registry entries, relationship categories, subtype entries, governance rules, and projection style rules should be modeled as `schema` nodes with specific subtypes. Those terms are part of the substrate vocabulary, not private implementation comments.

## Canonical vs Extension

Use canonical types for common substrate structure.

Use extensions for domain-specific modeling.

Example:

- `xananode:media` should be canonical.
- `museum:artifact` should be an extension.
- `biology:species` should be an extension unless future adoption proves otherwise.

nodes.title: Governance / Dispute Resolution
nodes.summary: governance/dispute-resolution.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Dispute Resolution

XanaNode treats disputes as knowledge, not merely failure.

A disputed claim should remain visible when appropriate, along with:

- who disputes it
- what evidence supports it
- what counterevidence challenges it
- what assumptions differ
- what sources are involved

Disputes can be represented with relationships such as:

- `contradicts`
- `contradicted_by`
- `disputes`
- `disputed_by`
- `alternative_to`
- `possible_match`
- `disputed_identity`

The goal is not instant consensus. The goal is navigable uncertainty.

nodes.title: Governance / Federation Rules
nodes.summary: governance/federation-rules.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Federation Rules

Federation connects independently authored substrates without forcing one source of truth.

A federation process should:

1. Load each substrate manifest.
2. Resolve each substrate's Git repository metadata.
3. Validate each substrate against declared schemas.
4. Preserve original node IDs and namespaces.
5. Detect likely overlaps.
6. Generate mapping relationships.
7. Preserve conflicts and uncertainty.
8. Avoid destructive merging.

Federation should create more knowledge, not erase local context.

## Never Collapse by Default

Two nodes that look similar should not automatically become one node.

Prefer:

- `same_entity`
- `possible_match`
- `disputed_identity`
- `equivalent_to`

depending on confidence and review status.

nodes.title: Governance / Moderation Model
nodes.summary: governance/moderation-model.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Moderation Model

XanaNode does not require one universal moderation authority.

Each substrate controls its own moderation rules.

Federation allows independently moderated substrates to be analyzed together while preserving their local decisions.

A substrate may choose to:

- approve nodes
- reject nodes
- deprecate claims
- preserve disputed claims
- hide low-confidence relationships
- publish merge reports

Moderation actions should themselves be traceable whenever possible.

nodes.title: Governance / README
nodes.summary: governance/README.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# XanaNode Governance

XanaNode governance defines how the protocol evolves while allowing independent substrates to remain sovereign.

The governance model is intentionally minimal:

1. Maintain a small stable core.
2. Allow namespaced extensions.
3. Preserve disagreement rather than deleting it.
4. Treat mappings, disputes, and merge results as knowledge.
5. Avoid centralizing truth in one moderation team.

Governance applies to the protocol repository. It does not dictate how every independent substrate must moderate its own content.

nodes.title: Governance / Schema Extension Rules
nodes.summary: governance/schema-extension-rules.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Schema Extension Rules

Substrates may define custom node and relationship types.

Custom types must be:

- namespaced
- versioned
- human-readable
- machine-readable
- preserved even when not understood
- optionally mapped to a core type

A valid extension should include:

- `id`
- `namespace`
- `type`
- `label`
- `purpose` or `meaning`
- `core: false`
- optional `extends`

Unknown extension types should not break import. They should be marked opaque and preserved.

nodes.title: Governance / Versioning Policy
nodes.summary: governance/versioning-policy.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Versioning Policy

XanaNode protocol files use semantic versioning where practical.

- Patch versions fix wording or validation mistakes.
- Minor versions add compatible types, fields, or examples.
- Major versions change compatibility expectations.

Schemas should be versioned independently from individual substrates.

Substrates should declare which core schema version they target.

Substrate history should be tracked in Git. Protocol version fields describe schema compatibility; Git commits describe concrete substrate revisions.

Example:

```json
{
  "schema_version": "xananode-core@0.5.0"
}
```

Breaking changes should go through the proposal process.

nodes.content:
# XanaNode Protocol Licensing

XanaNode uses a split license model.

## Documentation

Protocol documentation, explanatory text, specifications, governance text, proposals, examples, and README files are licensed under:

`CC-BY-4.0`

You may share and adapt the documentation with attribution.

## Schemas, Validators, And Reference Code

JSON Schemas, schema registries, validators, tooling, examples intended as machine-readable fixtures, and reference implementation code are licensed under:

`Apache-2.0`

You may use, modify, distribute, and implement these artifacts under the Apache License 2.0.

## Trademarks

The XanaNode name, XanaNode logo, and related brand assets are trademarks or trade dress of the XanaNode project. License grants for documentation, schemas, validators, and code do not grant trademark rights.

See [TRADEMARK.md](TRADEMARK.md).

nodes.title: Links / Implementations
nodes.summary: links/implementations.md is preserved as a raw protocol artifact in the XanaNode canonical substrate.
nodes.content:
# Implementations

Known or planned XanaNode implementations.

## Reference Implementations

- [XanaNode Hugo Theme](https://github.com/kingc95/xananode-hugo) - static-site renderer and graph viewer for XanaNode-compatible substrates.

## Repository Roles

- This repository defines the protocol, schemas, examples, governance, registry, and validation tooling.
- The Hugo repository implements rendering and browsing behavior for concrete substrates.
- Implementation-specific templates, assets, build scripts, and theme behavior should live in the Hugo repository.
- Protocol-level schema changes, canonical type changes, and validation rules should live in this repository.

## Possible Future Implementations

- desktop editor
- Obsidian plugin

Use proposals for changes that affect:

- canonical schemas

nodes.content:
# XNP-0001: Namespaces

Status: Accepted

## Summary

Defines how substrates and extension schemas avoid ID collisions.

## Motivation

XanaNode aims to support independently authored knowledge substrates that can later interoperate. This proposal records the design expectations for namespaces so implementations can converge without requiring centralized control.

## Requirements

- Must remain human-readable.
- Must remain machine-interpretable.
- Must preserve provenance.
- Must avoid destructive merging.
- Must allow independent substrate ownership.

## Open Questions

- What is the smallest useful implementation?
- Which parts are required for compatibility?
- Which parts should remain optional or experimental?
